title: Understanding the Oxidation Reaction in Combustion: A Review of the Scientific Evidence

lesson.learn:
Fire is a self-sustaining oxidation reaction. It needs fuel, oxygen, and heat, and once those ingredients reach the right threshold, the reaction can keep feeding itself. The energy released shows up as heat, light, and hot gases, which is why flames feel alive even though they are chemistry in motion.

The visible flame is not the whole reaction. What you see is the energetic byproduct of molecules rearranging themselves very quickly and releasing energy faster than it can spread away.
lesson.simpler: Fire happens when fuel reacts with oxygen fast enough to release heat and light. If the reaction keeps feeding itself, the flame continues.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Fire is combustion that keeps going because the reaction makes enough heat to sustain itself.
lesson.deeperDive:
Combustion works because heat helps break bonds in the fuel, producing reactive fragments that combine with oxygen. That process is exothermic, so the reaction releases more energy than it needs once it gets started. The fire triangle captures the essentials: fuel, oxygen, and heat.

Remove any one of those pieces and the flame dies. That simple rule is why fire safety, extinguishers, and controlled burners all focus on interrupting one part of the chain. The chemistry is fast, but the logic is beautifully direct.
lesson.funFact: A flame is partly gas-phase chemistry, not just “burning stuff.”

people: Antoine Lavoisier - explained combustion as oxidation
Michael Faraday - studied candle and flame chemistry
Sadi Carnot - helped shape thermodynamic thinking about heat
Josiah Willard Gibbs - advanced reaction energetics
places: Laboratories studying combustion - flame chemistry research
Fire safety training centers - practical combustion control
Everyday kitchens and engines - familiar combustion environments
years: 1770s - modern combustion chemistry begins to form
19th century - oxidation theory replaces older phlogiston ideas
20th century - flame chemistry becomes more quantitative
21st century - combustion science supports cleaner fuels
concepts: Combustion - rapid oxidation that releases energy
Oxidation - loss of electrons in a reaction
Ignition temperature - the point where combustion can start
Exothermic reaction - a reaction that releases heat
Chain reaction - a reaction that sustains itself
